coredata.dc:title: A DFT+U approach: Superior charge transfer characteristics and optoelectronic properties of GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface for improved hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Ullah, Farman
Mohamed, Norani Muti
Kait, Chong Fai
Ghani, Usman
Saheed, Mohamed Shuaib Mohamed
coredata.dc:description: The understanding on complex electronic structure and charge transfer characteristics is crucial in the development of heterostructure-based photocatalyst such as graphene quantum dots (GQD) and titanium dioxide (TiO2). Simulation studies will be useful in gaining valuable insight on the complex system at atomistic level. Herein, for the first time, a theoretically designed heterogeneous GQD modified TiO2 (110) interface model using Hubbard's modified first-principles density functional theory (DFT+U) is presented. The structural properties were simulated with Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof assisted generalized gradient approximation (GGA+PBE) and optoelectronic properties with Hubbard's modified (GGA+U) exchange correlation functional. The addition of GQD reduces the bandgap energy of the TiO2 rutile (110) surface from 2.95 eV to 1.86 eV, thereby improving the visible light response as it reduces the electron transition energy. Charge density difference map and Mulliken population analysis demonstrate frequent transfer of charges from GQD to the TiO2 surface, resulting in reduction of charge recombination rate. Moreover, the energy band edge estimation confirms a suitable band edge position in accordance with the redox potential of water. The collective effect of the heightened absorption of visible light and effective charge separation led to a significant photocatalytic performance of the hybrid photocatalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2 rutile (110) surface
Graphene quantum dots
Density functional theory (DFT) calculations
Optoelectronic properties
Charge transfer characteristics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Charge density difference display and Mulliken population analysis
Work function and energy level diagram

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Funding for this research work was provided by Ministry of Higher Education ( MOHE ), Malaysia and the Universiti Teknologi PETRONAS (UTP) under the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme ( FRGS ) (Grant No. FRGS/1/2019/STG07/UTP/01/1 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TiO2 rutile (110) surface model (a) side view (b) top view and (c) the ten (hexagonal) rings GQD model.
The GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) interface model with (a) un-optimized side view (b) optimized side view and (c) optimized top view.
The electronic band structures of (a) the TiO2 rutile (110) surface (b) the GQD and (c) the GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) interface models.
The density of states plots of (a) TiO2 rutile (110) surface (b) GQD molecule and (c) GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) interface models.

The 3-D charge density difference plots for (a) TiO2 rutile (110) and (b) GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface slabs. The Mulliken population analysis for (c) TiO2 rutile (110) and (d) GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface slab.
The simulated work function for (a) TiO2 rutile (110) (b) GQD and (c) GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface slabs.

(a) The simulated energy level diagram of the TiO2 rutile (110) surface, GQD molecule, and GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) heterostructure interface models (b) The band alignment diagram at the GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) interface.
The absorption vs wavelength display of the TiO2 rutile (110) surface and GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) interface models. Inset Fig. 8 depicts the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the GQD molecule.
The band edge positions, BGE, and WF of the TiO2 rutile (110), GQD and GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface slabs models.

head.ce:title: A DFT+U approach: Superior charge transfer characteristics and optoelectronic properties of GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface for improved hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Universiti Teknologi PETRONAS, 32610 Seri Iskandar, Perak Darul Ridzuan, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eDepartment of Mechanical Engineering, Universiti Teknologi PETRONAS, 32610 Seri Iskandar, Perak Darul Ridzuan, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The understanding on complex electronic structure and charge transfer characteristics is crucial in the development of heterostructure-based photocatalyst such as graphene quantum dots (GQD) and titanium dioxide (TiO2). Simulation studies will be useful in gaining valuable insight on the complex system at atomistic level. Herein, for the first time, a theoretically designed heterogeneous GQD modified TiO2 (110) interface model using Hubbard's modified first-principles density functional theory (DFT+U) is presented. The structural properties were simulated with Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof assisted generalized gradient approximation (GGA+PBE) and optoelectronic properties with Hubbard's modified (GGA+U) exchange correlation functional. The addition of GQD reduces the bandgap energy of the TiO2 rutile (110) surface from 2.95 eV to 1.86 eV, thereby improving the visible light response as it reduces the electron transition energy. Charge density difference map and Mulliken population analysis demonstrate frequent transfer of charges from GQD to the TiO2 surface, resulting in reduction of charge recombination rate. Moreover, the energy band edge estimation confirms a suitable band edge position in accordance with the redox potential of water. The collective effect of the heightened absorption of visible light and effective charge separation led to a significant photocatalytic performance of the hybrid photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing interest in circular economy necessitates viable and sustainable approaches in meeting global demands for energy while reducing the environmental pollutions. One such attractive approach but equally arduous is the harnessing of solar energy for the photocatalytic-based hydrogen production through water-splitting technique [1]. The important element for this technique is the photocatalyst such as titanium dioxide (TiO2), which is continuously to be researched even after almost five decades of its introduction. The primary focus is to improve its performance for light-harvesting applications through the understanding of atomic scale photoactivity. [2,3]. A key challenge that restricts the usefulness of the TiO2 is the wide bandgap, such as 3 eV for rutile phase. This confines the absorption of photons within ultraviolet (UV) region, accounting to only about 3–5% of the total sunlight spectra. Furthermore, the photogenerated electron-hole pair's high recombination rate is a subject of concern [4,5]. Doping of TiO2 with different metals, non-metals, and surface modification with other low bandgap semiconductors is generally employed to overcome the aforementioned obstacles. This approach narrows the bandgap, leading to absorption of photons in a wider spectrum while reducing the carrier's recombination rate to improve charge separation efficiency [6–10].

Another interesting approach is the surface modification of the TiO2 with graphene-based materials, demonstrating excellent optoelectronic characteristics. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is also an emergent class of zero-dimensional (0D) graphene-based material and is the latest addition to the family of nanocarbon materials [11,12]. GQDs are (nanosized) graphene fragments with an atomically thin graphitic plane of lateral dimensions < 10 nm and a thickness of < 2 nm (typically 1 or 2 layers). Due to the intensive quantum confinement and edge effect, GQDs own nonzero, size-dependent bandgaps, unique optoelectronic properties and have been recognized as one of the most exciting materials of the present decade for improving the TiO2 photocatalytic properties [11,13,14]. Moreover, GQDs display good photostability, high biocompatibility, potentially low-cost, environmentally friendly and facilitate the charge migration from the photoactive layer to the electron transport layer efficiently [12]. Therefore, GQD is an appropriate choice to adopt for the hybrid TiO2 based photocatalysts for light-harvesting applications.

Bayat et al. [15] investigated sulfur-nitrogen doped GQD modified TiO2 rutile nanorods as a photocatalyst for photocatalytic water splitting reaction with increased photocurrent by ∼ 4.5 times in comparison to the pure TiO2 electrode. Raghavan et al. [16]. reported the effect of GQDs loading on the different TiO2 nanostructures for photocatalytic H2 production and found GQD modified TiO2-based (P-25) nanocomposite has increased H2 production rate than pristine TiO2. R. Long [17] simulated the effect of GQD on the TiO2 rutile (110) surface through conventional DFT calculation and reported a reduced bandgap energy value of 1.1 eV for GQD/TiO2 composite in comparison to the 2.18 eV for the pure TiO2 (110) surface with efficient charge separation.

Although GQDs have emerged as an intriguing and promising co-catalyst option for TiO2 in a variety of photocatalytic applications, the performance of the GQD modified TiO2 hybrid photocatalysts is still insufficient and requires further development [18–20]. Primarily, the efficiency of the GQD modified TiO2 photocatalyst is continuously studied and improved through reduction of bandgap energy to an optimal range and charge carrier's recombination rate at the heterojunction interface. Most of the reported studies have focused upon the experimental aspects via developing different synthesis approaches for the preparation of GQD and subsequently, the adsorption on various morphologies of TiO2 [20]. Crucially, theoretical studies to complement the experimental outcomes in respect to complex electronic structure of the GQD modified TiO2 photocatalysts are still lacking.

Moreover, the recent literature also does not provide any information about the theoretically estimated band edge position and Mulliken population analysis at the interface of the hybrid GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) photocatalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen production. The scarcity of theoretical research on modeling the electronic structure and charge transfer characteristics of hybrid photocatalysts appears to be one of the major impediments to the further development of this class of hybrid photocatalysts. Computational simulation offers extensive information at atomic level in designing hybrid photocatalyst with an appropriate bandgap that absorbs more energy from visible solar spectrum. Therefore, systematic theoretical studies are necessary as it plays a vital role in understanding the effect of doping/surface modifications on the electronic structure and related properties. The computational modeling approach simulates and studies the behavior of a complex system by computer simulation and facilitates qualitative and quantitative interpretation of the data by investigating the parameters that will be difficult to approach experimentally [21,22]. Standard DFT calculations are generally implemented as a computational simulation method to calculate the materials' structural, electronic, and optical properties. However, for strongly correlated systems like transition metal oxides, it mostly underestimates the value of the bandgap energy and other related properties. Hubbard's assisted density functional theory (DFT+U) approach is a good alternative for standard DFT to overcome these problems by choosing a proper U value without much computational cost [23,24].

Therefore, for the first time, DFT+U simulation approach was implemented to analyze the effect of GQD addition to the TiO2 (110) surface by computing their effect on the electronic structure, interfacial charge transfer, and optical properties. The simulated electronic band structure and optical absorption coefficient estimation demonstrate a significant bandgap reduction with enhanced visible light absorption for the computationally designed GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) model. Furthermore, an efficient charge migration from GQD to the TiO2 rutile surface was observed at the interface of the GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) model. Moreover, the band edge potentials of the hybrid photocatalyst is still well positioned considering the redox potential of water, even though the bandgap was reduced significantly. The adopted GGA+U approach with Hubbard's potential U valued at 7.0 eV demonstrates accurate results for the bandgap energy calculation as compared to the conventional DFT approach. The designed theoretical model confirms the incorporation of GQD is an appropriate addition as a dopant element for the development of the TiO2 heterostructure photocatalyst for solar hydrogen production as reported elsewhere [15,16,25].

In this study, all the DFT-based theoretical calculations and computational models designing were carried out in Materials Studio software using Cambridge sequential total energy package (CASTEP) code with plane-wave total energy method [26]. The exchange-correlation interaction was implemented using the generalised gradient approximation (GGA) functional in conjunction with an ultrasoft pseudopotentials scheme in the Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE) format [27]. The GGA+PBE potential was employed to figure out the structural properties, while Hubbard's modified (GGA+U) potential was implemented to determine electronic and optical properties. It is important to highlight that with standard GGA+PBE potential, the bandgap energy for a system with strongly localized orbitals (d- and f-orbitals in transition metal oxides) may not be calculated in high accuracy [28,29]. The values are lowered up to 50% in comparison to the experimental values due to the less precise measurement of the strong Coulomb interactions between localised electrons in d/f orbitals. [29]. Previous theoretical studies suggest that DFT calculations assisted with Hubbard U correction provides accurate and efficient results, without paying a huge computational penalty [30,31]. Therefore, Hubbard's modified GGA+U potential method was adopted for the present study with a Hubbard-potential value of 7.0 eV for the localized Ti 3d electrons to obtain the bandgap energy value much closer to the experimental values. In particular, it should be noted that this approach achieves an excellent balance between the precision of the simulation and the amount of computational processing resources that are accessible. In addition, the DFT-D method within the Grimme scheme was also adopted to consider the long-range van der Waals (vdW) forces and dispersive intermolecular interactions [32]. The noncovalent vdW bonds between the layer-separated electrons and holes establish bound exciton state at the GQD-TiO2 interface and thus the interfacial charge transfer happens via these bound excitons between two neighbouring transient dipoles due to Coulomb's interactions [33,34]. It is worth noting that these interactions arise as a result of quantum fluctuations generated by the induced transient dipoles, and that simple quasi-classical models have been used to determine the magnitude of the resulting force. [35].

Therefore, all the designed GQD and TiO2 rutile (110) surface models were studied with the spin-polarized DFT calculations including Hubbard U and vdW dispersion forces with the DFT+U+vdW approach. A (4 × 2) 8-layered TiO2 rutile (110) surface was built from the optimized bulk TiO2 unit cell by choosing the bottom four layers fixed and the upper four layers relaxed in the surface slab model. Enough vacuum space of 20 Å is added perpendicular to the TiO2 rutile (110) surface to avoid the interaction between periodic images. The atomic positioning and cell parameters were relaxed with residual forces set to less than 0.03 eV/Å and atomic displacement set to 2 × 10- 3 Å. The ultrasoft pseudopotential approach was applied to the electronic configuration of Ti; 3s2 3p6 3d2 4s2, C; 2s2 2p2, and O; 2s2 2p4 to speed up the computational runs. For numerical sample integration, a 3 × 2 × 2-k-point mesh with the Monkhorst–Pack method was employed across the Brillouin zone, with a valence electron cut-off energy of 300 eV. The convergence parameters were chosen with the self-consistent field (SCF) iterations tolerance of 2 × 10−6 eV/atom and the stress on the atom was adjusted below 0.1 GPa. The stability of the GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) interface was determined from the adsorption energy of the GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) heterostructure according to Eq. (1),
(1)
E
a d s
=
E
G Q D @ T i O
2
−
(
E
G Q D
+
E
T i
O 2
)
where E
GQD@TiO reflects the amount of total energy of a GQD molecule adsorbed on the TiO2 rutile (110) surface, denotes the total energy of the GQD molecule, and
E
T i
O 2
shows the total energy of the TiO2 rutile (110) surface.

Fig. 1 (a, b) displays the side and top view of the computationally designed pure TiO2 rutile (110) surface model. A (4 × 2) extended supercell model of the eight atomic layers was initially designed from the bulk TiO2 rutile unit cell. Bulk TiO2 rutile unit cell adopts a tetragonal Bravais lattice geometry with P42 mnm space group and contains two titanium (Ti) and four oxygen (O) atoms. Each cation (Ti4+) in the TiO2 rutile unit cell coordinated octahedrally to the six anions (O2−), while each anion (O2−) coordinated to the three cations (Ti4+) with OTi3 pattern. The crystallographic parameters include the two lattice parameters "a" and "c," as well as the oxygen fractional coordinate "u," where u is the internal parameter expressed in fractional units of the crystal structure. The Ti atoms are positioned at (0, 0, 0) and (½, ½, ½) whereas the oxygen atoms are positioned at (u, u, 0; ½ + u, ½ + u, ½) where, u = 0.304937. The calculated optimized lattice parameters for the bulk TiO2 rutile unit cell are a = b = 4.56 Å, c = 2.93 Å, and are in good agreement with the previously reported theoretical values of a = b = 4.58 Å, c = 2.95 Å [36], illustrating the adopted calculation method is suitable for the structural properties calculation. The TiO2 rutile (110) surface is characterized by a slab model using periodic boundary conditions (Fig. 1 (a, b), side and top views, respectively). As displayed in Fig. 1(a), the rutile (110) surface shows two types of Ti atoms, referred to as five-fold (Ti5f) and six-fold (Ti6f) atoms along with two types of oxygen atoms, named plane-O3f and bridging-O2f atoms. It is important to highlight that TiO2 rutile (110) surface layer involves a tri-atomic layer with two in-plane Ti, two in-plane O atoms, and two bridging O atoms above and below the central Ti-O layer. The selected stoichiometric surface slab has dimension of 8.877 Å × 12.993 Å and vacuum depth up to 20 Å, containing a total of 123 atoms with 75 O and 48 Ti atoms.

The GQD model was designed with 32 carbon and 14 hydrogen atoms, configured in a ten fused benzene rings symmetry as demonstrated in Fig. 1(c). The hydrogen atoms were only saturated at the edges of the GQD molecule. The structure was then optimized to obtain the ground state energy configuration. The geometry optimization of the GQD structured model demonstrates a CC bond length of 1.42 Å and agree well with the previously reported theoretical values [37].

The GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface slab model was designed by placing the GQD molecule at the top surface of the TiO2 surface slab. The GQD molecule interacts with the TiO2 substrate through the long-range vdW interactions. The vdW dispersion interactions are intrinsically quantum mechanical interactions and are based upon the quantum fluctuations of the induced transient dipoles [30,38-40]. Three different GQD models with four, seven, and ten hexagonal fused benzene rings configurations were structured on the TiO2 surface slab. The adsorption energies of all the designed models were computed to determine the most stable configuration having more negative adsorption energy. The designed ten benzene rings GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) interface model demonstrates the most stable configuration with the estimated adsorption energy of −1.69 eV. On the other hand, the four and seven rings adsorbed GQD models own the adsorption energy of −1.29 eV and −1.53 eV with a comparatively lesser stable configuration. The physiosorbed ten rings GQD molecule on the TiO2 rutile (110) surface with unoptimized and optimized geometries is shown in Fig. 2 (a, b). Four and seven rings GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface models are displayed in supplementary data (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2) for comparison. After geometry optimization of the hybrid model, the physiosorbed GQD molecule slightly altered its geometry with the edges of the molecule turns down towards the bridging-O2f atoms of the TiO2 surface whereas the central part bends up to illustrate the strong interaction between GQD and TiO2 surface (side and top views in Fig. 2 (b, c)). The offset of the GQD molecule at the edges is mainly related to the greater electronegativity (3.44) of the oxygen atom within the TiO2 lattice as compared to the carbon atom (2.55) of the GQD molecule. In contrast, the TiO2 surface structure remained unchanged due to its rigorous inorganic semiconductor nature, as previously documented [17]. The optimized averaged separation of GQD from the TiO2 surface is approximately 2.27 Å. Through Coulomb interactions, electrons and holes create bound exciton states, hence interfacial charge transfer happens via these bound exciton states between two adjacent transient dipoles at the GQD@TiO2 interface [30,33,34,38-40]. The calculated adsorption energy Eads of the GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) heterostructure is −1.69 eV, indicating a good stable configuration with significant interaction between GQD and TiO2 rutile (110) surface.

The photocatalytic performance of the TiO2 photocatalyst is strongly dependent on the electronic structure and its related properties. The effect of the GQD incorporation on the electronic properties of the TiO2 rutile (110) surface model is investigated by computing the electronic band structures and density of states of TiO2 rutile (110) surface and GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) interface model, as shown in Fig. 3 . The blue dashed line at zero-point energy shows the Fermi energy level. The calculated bandgap energy (Eg) for pure TiO2 rutile (110) surface is 2.95 eV with Hubbard U value of 7 eV, as displayed in Fig. 3(a), and is in close agreement with the experimentally known value of 3 eV for rutile TiO2 [41,42]. Both conduction band minimum (CBM) and valence band maximum (VBM) present at ΓZ line indicates the direct bandgap of TiO2 rutile (110) [43]. The calculated bandgap energy for the designed GQD model is valued at 2.03 eV (Fig. 3(b)), whereas it is 1.86 eV for the GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface model (Fig. 3(c)). The addition of the GQD to the TiO2 rutile (110) surface modifies their electronic structures by incorporating the new impurity states in the bandgap region and thus reduce the bandgap significantly as compared to the pure TiO2 rutile [44]. It is noteworthy to highlight that the bandgap reduction occurred mainly due to the CBM downward shift towards the Fermi energy level. New carbon impurity states appearing in the bottom edge of the CB region enforced the CBM to move downward and thus resulting in an effective bandgap reduction of the TiO2 surface. The reduction of the bandgap energy results in easier transition of electrons from VB to CB in the GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface as compared to TiO2 (110) surface. This would result in the redshift of the absorption edge towards the longer wavelength region [45]. More visible light photons (in the longer wavelength region) will thus be absorbed from the solar spectrum and hence, the photoexcited electron-hole generation increases at the GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) interface in comparison to the pure TiO2 (110) surface slab. Moreover, the induced impurity states also trap the electrons to efficiently separate the electron-hole pairs and reduce the charge carrier recombination rate. The photoexcited electron-hole separation thus occurs more efficiently at the GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) interface, implying that the photocatalytic performance of the GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface will be stronger than that of the TiO2 (110) slab [15,16,25].

The projected density of states (PDOS) of the pure TiO2 rutile (110) surface, GQD, and GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) interface with predicted lowest-energy structure are presented in Fig. 4 (a-c). The corresponding PDOS plot of the pure TiO2 surface is calculated for comparison and to clearly understand the influence of the GQDs on the electronic structure of the TiO2 surface. The bandgap reduction is also confirmed by the density of states (DOS) plots, as shown in Fig. 4. The calculated total density of states (TDOS) plot for pure TiO2 surface also illustrate the bandgap energy value of 2.95 eV, as was previously estimated from the band structure plot. The VB of pure TiO2 rutile surface constitutes mostly from O2p states with a lesser portion of Ti3d states while CB takes more Ti3d states with a smaller contribution from O2p states, as shown in Fig. 4(a). A cluster of bands appears within the energy range from 3.0 to 8.0 eV above the Fermi energy level in the CB region which mainly corresponds to the Ti-3d states.

The simulated PDOS plot of the GQD molecule is screened in Fig. 4(b). The energy bandgap estimation shows the value of 2.03 eV for the designed GQD model. Both in the VB and CB portion of the PDOS profile, a cluster of states corresponding to the C2p states predominantly appeared with a lesser contribution of the C2s states in the lower VB part of the plot. The PDOS diagram of the GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface is shaped in Fig. 4(c). It is obvious from the plot that with the addition of GQD, the ingap-state bands were introduced into the TiO2 surface thereby reducing the bandgap energy up to 1.86 eV. All the ingap states came from the C atoms with a wide energy range distribution. For the GQD@TiO2 surface, the dominating character in the VB range belongs to O2p+C2p states, positioned just below the Fermi energy level, from 0 to −5.7 eV along with significant hybridization from Ti3d states. The new impurity C2p states were also appeared (with smaller contribution) and observed to be deep in the VB energy range from −6 to −10 eV due to the addition of GQD dopant. On the other hand, from the CB portion of the PDOS plot, a primary contribution is evident from the Ti3d states in conjunction with a significant hybridization of the C2p states of GQD. It is notable to see the appearance of the ingap-state bands mainly corresponding to the C2p states of the GQD lattice with an efficient contribution in both VB and CB regions. The addition of the C2p states to the TiO2 lattice pushes the CBM towards the Fermi energy level by introducing smaller shoulder peaks of the C2p states, appeared just at the bottom of the CB within the bandgap region as compared to the pure TiO2. The bandgap is thus efficiently reduced to a value of 1.86 eV which also agrees well with the requirement of the bandgap narrowing without reducing the photocatalytic ability of the material. Moreover, the corresponding orbital peaks sharpness in the VB and CB portion also enhanced noticeably due to the addition of C2p states, and thus the density of states increases, as evident form the PDOS plot of the GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge density difference display and Mulliken population analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To elaborate on the interfacial charge transfer at the GQD@TiO2 interface, the three-dimensional (3D) charge density difference maps for pure TiO2 (110) surface and hybrid GQD@TiO2 interface were plotted as shown in Fig. 5 (a, b). For pure TiO2 (110) surface slab, the 3D charge density difference map was computed by choosing only the “Atomic densities” display, as illustrated in Fig. 5(a). To allocate the charge accumulation and depletion regions, an isosurface valued at 4 × 10−4 e/Å3 was employed by choosing blue-colored isosurfaces for the electronic charge accumulation regions while yellow-colored isosurfaces for the space charge depletion regions. The blue isosurfaces appeared around O atoms, as O atoms secure more electron density, whereas, the yellow isosurfaces appeared around Ti atoms, due to the loss of electron density. The accumulation of more electrons around the O atoms is due to the greater electronegativity of the O atom (3.44) as compared to the Ti atoms (1.54).

For the GQD@TiO2 (110) surface slab model, the charge density difference map was displayed by choosing the “Both atomic densities and sets of atoms” display, as presented in Fig. 5(b). The physiosorbed GQD molecule interacts with the TiO2 surface through vdW interactions. The charge redistribution and direction of the charge transfer can thus be demonstrated through the 3D charge density difference map. The charge transfer occurs between the induced transient dipoles through the formation of bound exciton states at the interface of the GQD and TiO2 surface. The appearance of yellow space regions around the set of the GQD atoms points out that the charge depletion occurs around the GQD molecule, whereas, the appearance of the blue-colored isosurfaces around the TiO2 atoms at the top surface layers illustrate the charge accumulation around the TiO2 atoms. It is thus clear from the 3D charge density difference map that significant electron transfer occurs from GQD to the TiO2 surface. Furthermore, it is also demonstrating that the interfacial charge transfer mainly occurs through the top surface layers of the TiO2 (110) surface and GQD molecule. The charge redistribution due to the vdW interactions results in a notable charge transfer from the GQD to the TiO2 surface [30,38-40]. The development of the charge accumulation and depletion regions creates an electric field at the GQD@TiO2 (110) interface and thus efficiently separate the e −/h + pair to lower the charge recombination rate. Moreover, the development of a strong interaction due to the efficient charge transfer at the GQD@TiO2 interface will ultimately result in the development of a more stable photocatalyst. The work function estimation of the GQD@TiO2 (110) surface also validates the charge density difference predictions of the charge migration from GQD to the TiO2 (110) surface.

The Mulliken charge analysis simulation approach was also employed to better understand the mechanism of the charge transfer and the electronic changes associated with the addition of GQD molecules into the TiO2 systems. The concept of the Mulliken charge analysis is based upon the atoms in molecules (AIM) approach by defining the atomic regions within molecules and thus, spatially resolving the minimum electron density of the system [46]. As displayed in Fig. 5(c), the estimated average Mulliken charge values appear ∼ +1.18 for the Ti5f atoms and ∼ −0.67 for O2f atoms (encircled by red-coloured dashed line) in the top surface layer of the designed TiO2 rutile (110) surface model. It is notable to mention that the same type of atoms on a surface may also have different charges due to the different coordination of the atoms, thus shows different chemical reactivity. The estimated averaged negative charge values for O atoms mean that O atoms gain the electrons, whereas the averaged positive charge values for Ti atoms illustrate the loss of electrons. The O atoms acquire more electrons than Ti because of the greater electronegativity of O atoms (3.44) in comparison to the Ti atoms (1.54) [47]. The results are also consistent with the simulated 3D charge density difference map and work function analysis.

For the designed GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface slab, the corresponding O2f atoms (Fig. 5(d)) specifies a Mulliken charge of −0.73, whereas the ridge Ti5f atoms demonstrate a charge of +1.24. The estimated values for both O and Ti atoms differs around 0.06, as compared to the pure TiO2 surface model. Moreover, the Mulliken charge (averaged) estimation of the C atoms in GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) interface model signifies a value of +0.031 in comparison to the value of +0.024 of the GQD model. The Mulliken charge analysis provided a way to predict the reactivity of a given atom within the designed surface model. Also, the Mulliken population analysis indicates significant charge transfer from GQD to the TiO2 rutile (110) surface and thus establishing a stable interface between the interacting GQD and TiO2 surface layers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Work function and energy level diagram
ce:section.ce:para:
The charge transfer descriptions at the GQD and TiO2 rutile (110) interface were also studied through computation of the work functions for the designed TiO2 rutile (110) surface, GQD molecule, and GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) interface models. Work function demonstrates the minimum (threshold) energy desirable for an electron to jump outside of the solid into a field-free zone [48]. The work function is simulated as the energy difference between a stationary electron in the vacuum (E
vac ) and Fermi energy level (E
F ), as reported by Guosheng Shao [49] and is given below:
(2)
Φ
=
E
v a c
− −
E F

It is notable to mention that E
vac is assumed to be zero. The estimated work functions of the TiO2 rutile (110) surface, GQD, and GQD@TiO2 interface were valued at 5.85, 4.83, and 5.33 eV as portrayed in Fig. 6 (a-c). The electrostatic potential work function map illustrates the lower work function of the GQD (4.83 eV) as compared to the TiO2 rutile (110) surface (5.85 eV) enforce the charges to move from GQD to the TiO2 surface. The charge transfer continues until the Fermi energy levels of the GQD and TiO2 surface are aligned, as displayed in the band alignment diagram in Fig. 7 (b). The re-positioning of the Fermi level, VB, and CB result in band bending (band offset) and established built-in electric field. This condition facilitates the increased photogenerated electrons transfer by decreasing the photogenerated electrons/holes recombination rate [50,51]. Furthermore, the GQD@TiO2 heterojunction establishes a type-II band alignment with VBM positioned more negative and CBM positioned more positive corresponding to the water oxidation and reduction potential. The suitable band edge positions (both CBM and VBM) of the designed GQD@TiO2 heterojunction photocatalyst plays a dominant role to facilitate the redox reaction more efficiently, thus enhanced the photocatalytic activities by assisting the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER). It is interesting to note that even if a semiconductor is capable to absorb efficient solar light, the band edge energies must be positioned precisely in order to emphasize the HER and OER [52]. In principle, a photocatalytic reaction is based on two half reactions: reduction (HER) and oxidation (OER). The electrons in CB may initiate the reduction reaction, and the position of CB determines the reduction capability; the holes in VB may launch the oxidation reaction, and the position of VB determines the oxidation capability. For an efficient water splitting reaction, the CB energy level should be positioned more negative than the redox potential of H2/H + (0 V vs NHE, pH = 7), while the VB should be located more positive than the redox potential of H2O/O2 (1.23 V vs NHE, pH = 7). The HER (H2/H +) and OER (O2/H2O) reactions in a PEC cell are formulated as given below:
(3)
E l e c t r o n − h o l e
p a i r
f o r m a t i o n
o n
T i
O 2
: T i
O 2
+ 4 h v → 4
e −
+ 4
h +
(4)
O E R a t
T i
O 2
P h o t o a n o d e :
(
E
H 2
o
o /
o 2
= − 123
V
N H E
)
2
H 2
+ 4
h +
→ 4
H +
O 2
(5)
H E R
a t
P t
e l e c t r o d e
:
(
E
H + /
H 2
o
= + 0.00
V
N H E
)
4
e −
→ 2
H 2
(6)
O v e r a l l
r e d o x
r e a c t i o n
:
H 2
O + 4 h v → 2
H 2
+
O 2

To elaborate the phenomenon more clearly, the energy level diagram based on the simulated band edge positions of the TiO2 rutile (110) surface, GQD molecule, and GQD@TiO2 (110) interface models is illustrated in Fig. 7(a).

The various band edge positions of the GQD and TiO2 surface (Fig. 7(a)) demonstrates that the charges can be effectively transferred from GQD to the TiO2 (110) surface. The band alignment/band bending has a key role in the development of an efficient heterostructure photocatalyst, by decreasing the recombination rate to effectively separate the charge carriers [53]. Furthermore, the resultant GQD@TiO2 heterostructure arranged the VBM, CBM, and Fermi level at different positions in comparison to their constituents after the formation of the heterojunction. Overall, the combined effect of the band offset and built-in electric field favors the effective transfer of the electrons and holes that will suppress the charge carrier's recombination rate and will principally enhance the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalyst effectively [50,54]. Table 1 summarizes the values of the VBM, CBM, bandgap center, Fermi level, BGE, and WF of the TiO2 rutile (110), GQD, and GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) surface slabs models. Table. 1 .

The optical absorption characteristics are crucial in determining the photocatalytic performance of a semiconductor photocatalyst and are strongly linked to the electronic band structure of the material [55]. An efficient photocatalyst absorbs the dominant fraction of the solar visible spectrum. The optical absorption (UV–vis) coefficient plot for pure and GQD modified TiO2 rutile (110) models is shown in Fig. 8 . The pure TiO2 rutile (110) surface responds mainly in the UV spectra within the wavelength range between 200 and 380 nm as shown in Fig. 8. The absorption profile portrays the depth of specific wavelength penetration into the material before being absorbed. The computed result is also consistent with the previously reported theoretical calculations [56,57]. The absorption peak corresponds to the transition of the electrons from O2p states at the valence band to the Ti3d states at the conduction band. The wide bandgap energy restricts pure TiO2 rutile (110) surface to absorbs principally the ultraviolet radiation, and thus reducing their application range. The inset Fig. 8 corresponds to the UV–vis absorption plot for the GQD molecule with a prominent absorption around 330–350 nm wavelength range.

Incorporation of the GQDs into the TiO2 rutile (110) model alters the electronic structure by reducing the bandgap and extending the absorption edge from UV range towards lower energy (longer wavelength) visible range with a significant redshift, similar to our earlier work [35]. The redshift of the optical absorption edge is in generous agreement with the band structure analysis as the optical properties of a semiconductor photocatalyst originated from the electronic transitions. Due to the energy bandgap reduction from 2.95 eV for pure TiO2 to a value of 1.86 eV for GQD@TiO2 surface, the absorption band edge of the GQD@TiO2 (110) surface shifted up to 550 nm in the visible range of the solar spectrum. The designed GQD@TiO2 (110) photocatalyst model is thus screening a wider fraction of the solar spectrum. The visible portion of the solar spectrum contribute up to 43–45% in the total solar spectrum. Therefore, an enhanced absorption in the visible region will be beneficial, thereby improving the performance of the PEC cell [58,59]. It is important to highlight that the theoretically calculated red-shift values generally appear smaller than the experimental values due to the reason those theoretical calculations do not consider the secondary excitation and indirect transition effect. Also, the effect of the magnetic dipole and electric quadrupole is not considered in this type of calculation. These calculations are based upon the first excitation and direct transition between the occupied and unoccupied states [55]. Therefore, the theoretical calculations are used as approximate calculations in providing only the qualitative explanation and are not enough for quantitative research demands.

The goal of the research study was to understand the effect of GQDs addition to the TiO2 rutile (110) surface by computing their effect on the electronic structure, interfacial charge transfer, and optical properties with the DFT simulation technique. Hubbard's modified GGA+U exchange correlational functional was employed to calculate the optoelectronic properties. The estimated bandgap energy value for pure TiO2 rutile (110) surface was 2.95 eV, closely approaching the experimental value of 3 eV and thus endorsing the accuracy of the adopted Hubbard's modified approach. The computationally assessed results suggest that incorporation of GQDs induces new impurity energy states in the band structure of the TiO2 and hence the bandgap energy of the TiO2 rutile (110) surface model was narrowed down effectively up to 1.86 eV, to absorb wider spectrum in visible light. Charge transfer characteristics are also improved remarkably, as significant number of charges are drifted from the GQD to the TiO2 surface, and thus reducing the recombination rate as demonstrated by the charge density difference display and Mulliken population analysis. Furthermore, the energy band estimation confirms the suitable band edge position for the hybrid GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) heterostructure photocatalyst corresponding to the water's redox potential. Overall, the computational results provides a significant insight on the GQD@TiO2 rutile (110) heterostructure as a feasible photocatalyst with significantly reduced bandgap, efficient charge separation, and suitable band edge position. Moreover, the research findings provide new perspective and valuable guidance for future development of this exciting hybrid photocatalyst for photocatalytic hydrogen production.

Farman Ullah: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Visualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Validation. Norani Muti Mohamed: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Chong Fai Kait: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Validation. Usman Ghani: Software, Writing – review & editing. Mohamed Shuaib Mohamed Saheed: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding for this research work was provided by Ministry of Higher Education (MOHE), Malaysia and the Universiti Teknologi PETRONAS (UTP) under the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS) (Grant No. FRGS/1/2019/STG07/UTP/01/1).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2022.101952.
